3: The organic food market has increased dramatically in recent years (Sahota, 2016), and the success of organic foods is common to many countries in the world. In Italy, the consumption value of organic foods increased by 11% on average each year from 2003 to 2015 (ISMEA, 2016). This trend has been particularly favourable for dairy sector, and was likely due to the special attention consumers

give to environmental sustainability and animal welfare (Bravo et al., 2013). In European Union, organic production is based on the council Regulations No 834/2007 and No 889/2008, which include several aspects intended to protect and to improve the environment. In organic farming, pesticides and synthetic fertil- izers are banned, animal operations without land are not permitted and there are strict constrains to stocking rate. Meier et al. (2015) reviewed the beneficial effects of organic productions on the environment and they pointed out that the environmental impacts are always lower than those of the conventional system, when in reference to the unit of area per year. However, the results are

inconsistent when referring product units. One of the main reasons behind these conclusions is the lower productivity of organic sys- items (Seufert et al., 2012), which has been estimated to be 10-15% on average for milk by Pirlo (2016).

Beginning with the first article by Cederberg and Mattsson (2000) and until today, at least 10 life cycle assessment (LCA) studies comparing the environmental performances of organic and conventional milk production have been published (their main characteristics are reported in Table 1). Studies concerning the ef- fect of production systems on emissions of greenhouse gases (GHG) and acidifying and eutrophying substances per unit of milk pro- duced are conflicting. However, there is a wide consensus on the better environmental performances of organic milk production per unit of area occupied.

The productive and environmental performances of organic farming are more sensitive to environmental condition than those of conventional farming. Therefore it is important to consider that most of the studies in Table 1 were done in Central or Northern Europe. In all the organic farms examined in the studies, animals had access to pasture; maize silage were rarely grown on organic farms and, if present, its contribution was very low. In Italy, milk production is concentrated in the northern plain regions, mainly in Lombardy, that contributes approximately 35% of Italian milk pro- duced. The climatic (little rain precipitations in the summer) and economic (high land value) conditions of this area are not favour- able for permanent grasslands or grazing. Thus, nearly all cattle are kept indoors year round and have little access to pasture and maize silage is the main forage crop. One survey (Scossa and Tripaldi,

2000) showed that these characteristics are also common to organic farms. A study investigating the environmental perfor- mance of organic dairy farms in 6 European countries also pointed out that time spent on pasture and the contribution of permanent grassland were much lower in Italian dairy farms than in other countries (Hietala et al., 2015). Liang et al. (2017) showed that a high contribution of pasture at the expense of grain for feed pro- duction is associated with a reduced milk production and to an increase of GHG emissions per kilogram of energy corrected milk. Aguirre-Villegas et al. (2017) also indicated that any environmental benefits from grazing are small when environmental conditions are unfavourable. However, while maize silage-based systems are associated with a higher feed production (Egan et al., 2015), they are also associated with high energy consumptions and GHG emissions (Kim et al., 2014).

Considering the specificity of the environmental context and of farming practices in norther Italy, in comparison to those of the northern and central European countries, the objective of this study was to evaluate the impact of organic milk production on global warming potential (GWP), acidification potential (ACP) and eutrophication potential (EUP) in comparison with the impact of conventional milk production system. It was hypothesized that the high contribution of maize to diet composition could reduce the disadvantage of organic milk production also referred to unit of product. This study also aimed to evaluate the effect of the farm variability on environmental performances.
Table 1 Main characteristics of LCA studies comparing conventional and organic milk production in Europe.

The climatic (little rain precipitations in the summer) and economic (high land value) conditions of this area are not favour- able for permanent grasslands or grazing. Thus, nearly all cattle are kept indoors year round and have little access to pasture and maize silage is the main forage crop. One survey (Scossa and Tripaldi,

2000) showed that these characteristics are also common to organic farms. A study investigating the environmental perfor- mance of organic dairy farms in 6 European countries also pointed out that time spent on pasture and the contribution of permanent grassland were much lower in Italian dairy farms than in other countries (Hietala et al., 2015). 11: In this study, an LCA methodology, adopted in accordance with ISO (2006a,b), was chosen because it allows for an analysis of the environmental impact of a product or a service throughout its life cycle by considering different impact categories. An LCA study consists of four main steps: 1) definition of the goal and scope, 2) life cycle inventory analysis, 3) life cycle impact assessment and 4) interpretation of results. In the first step, the study, system boundaries, functional units and assumptions are defined. In the second step, inputs and outputs are reported. In the third step, potential environmental results are assessed on the basis of the life cycle inventory results. Finally, the results are analysed and inter- preted in the fourth step.
12: <header>2.1. Goal of the study and decision context </header>

The goal of the study was to compare the environmental effects of milk production obtained from two different systems: conven- tional and organic. In addition, it aimed to identify the variables that had the influence on the environmental performances in both systems. Organic milk production system differs from conventional system because it must be in compliance with the Council Regulations No 834/2007 and No 889/2008, which impose some limitations regarding the stocking rate and the amount of pur- chased feeds as well as forbidding synthetic fertilizers and pesticides.
13: The system boundaries considered in the study were from cradle to farm gate (Fig. 1). All operations pertaining to forage production and animal feeding were considered. The analysis took into account indirect consumptions and emissions derived from the production of chemical fertilizers, seeds, and pesticides; produc- tion, transport, and processing of purchased feeds; fossil fuel extraction; and maintenance of agricultural equipment and ma- chines. Synthetic fertilizers and pesticides were not present in the organic system. The study considered soil carbon emissions or sink that occur when a permanent or temporary pasture is converted to an annual crop or when a pasture is restored. In addition, in the case of the conventional system, background data concerning soybean from Brazil were considered for carbon emission caused by land use change. The study also took into account the emission of N

Fig. 1. Cradle-to-farm-gate system boundaries of the production of conventional or organic milk, expressed either on 1 kg of fat protein corrected milk or on 1 ha of cultivated area. Pesticides and chemical fertilizers are not present in organic system.
compounds and CH4 due to manure storage, the emissions of N and P from manure application, and CH4 from enteric fermentation.

Two functional units were defined for this study. To estimate the environmental effect associated with production mass, the first functional unit of 1 kg of fat and protein corrected milk (FPCM), as defined by Gerber et al. (2011), was used; the second functional unit was 1 ha of occupied area, which was adopted to estimate the impact intensity on the region where the farms were located.

15: Milk production is a multifunctional process that generates meat from the culled cows and calves as a byproduct. In this study, we adopted three different criteria: no-allocation, economic allo- cation, and physical allocation according to the guidelines of FIL-IDF (2015).
16: Data were collected from six organic farms and eight conven- tional dairy farms located in the Lombardy plain region. The organic farms are suppliers of a large dairy company that processes and markets organic milk in Italy. The relatively small sample size was due to the limited number of organic dairy farms in Italy [57,206 cows according to SINAB (2016)]. During the same period, information from eight conventional farms was included in the data base at our research centre and was compared to information on the organic farms in the study. Primary data refer to data collected from the year 2015 and concern feed production and livestock management. These data were collected at each farm by means of questionnaires that included the following items: number of animals for each category; housing system; manure collection, handling and storage system; consumption and type of fertilizers and pesticides; diet of different categories; milk production and composition; amount and type of purchased feeds; and consump- tion of electricity and fuels. Secondary data mainly refer to infor- mation regarding Italian specific conditions and calculation methodologies. The Ecoinvent database (v.2.0; Swiss Centre for Life Cycle Inventories, 2013) and the European Reference Life Cycle Database (European Commission, JRC-IES, 2013) were used for the production of off-farm feeds. The method used to estimate the CO2 emissions from electric consumptions, the combustion of fuels for agricultural operations, and the production of synthetic fertilizers, pesticides, and plastic materials are described in Pirlo and Carè (2013).

The methodology used for estimating emissions of GHG from enteric fermentation, manure management and manure applica- tion was based on IPCC (2006) and is described in details in Pirlo and Carè (2013). Nitrogen excreted by both mature and young cattle was estimated according to IPCC (2006) guidelines, and accomplished by subtracting the N retained by the animals to the N ingested. Nitrogen oxide emissions from manure management and applications were calculated according to Cóndor (2011). Direct soil N20 emissions were calculated from the N applied in the soil with organic and inorganic fertilizers, crop residues, and N-fixing crops, adjusted for the N lost by volatilization. The IPCC (2006) was also adopted to consider indirect N20 emissions such as those that occur following N leaching and runoff, and following redeposition of volatilized gases to soil and water. Emission factors of NH3 from chemical fertilizers, manure storage and spreading and animal housing and nitrate waterborne emissions were obtained from Cóndor et al. (2011). Phosphorus waterborne emissions were

estimated according to the budget farm-gate methodology re- ported in Dalgaard et al. (2008). Carbon sequestration was esti- mated according to Dollé et al. (2015). The characterization factors used in the life cycle impact assessment for GWP were 1, 28, and 265 CO2eq for CO2, CH4, and N20 respectively (IPCC, 2013); for ACP, they were 1.88, 0.7, and 1.0 SO2eq for NH3, NOx, and SO2 respec- tively; and for EUP, they were 0.35, 0.1, 3.06, and 0.13 PO eq for NH3, NO3, P, and NOx respectively (Guinée et al., 2002).
17: Data were collected from six organic farms and eight conven- tional dairy farms located in the Lombardy plain region. The organic farms are suppliers of a large dairy company that processes and markets organic milk in Italy. The relatively small sample size was due to the limited number of organic dairy farms in Italy [57,206 cows according to SINAB (2016)]. During the same period, information from eight conventional farms was included in the data base at our research centre and was compared to information on the organic farms in the study. Primary data refer to data collected from the year 2015 and concern feed production and livestock management. These data were collected at each farm by means of questionnaires that included the following items: number of animals for each category; housing system; manure collection, handling and storage system; consumption and type of fertilizers and pesticides; diet of different categories; milk production and composition; amount and type of purchased feeds; and consump- tion of electricity and fuels. Secondary data mainly refer to infor- mation regarding Italian specific conditions and calculation methodologies. The Ecoinvent database (v.2.0; Swiss Centre for Life Cycle Inventories, 2013) and the European Reference Life Cycle Database (European Commission, JRC-IES, 2013) were used for the production of off-farm feeds. The method used to estimate the CO2 emissions from electric consumptions, the combustion of fuels for agricultural operations, and the production of synthetic fertilizers, pesticides, and plastic materials are described in Pirlo and Carè (2013).

The methodology used for estimating emissions of GHG from enteric fermentation, manure management and manure applica- tion was based on IPCC (2006) and is described in details in Pirlo and Carè (2013). Nitrogen excreted by both mature and young cattle was estimated according to IPCC (2006) guidelines, and accomplished by subtracting the N retained by the animals to the N ingested. Nitrogen oxide emissions from manure management and applications were calculated according to Cóndor (2011). Direct soil N20 emissions were calculated from the N applied in the soil with organic and inorganic fertilizers, crop residues, and N-fixing crops, adjusted for the N lost by volatilization. The IPCC (2006) was also adopted to consider indirect N20 emissions such as those that occur following N leaching and runoff, and following redeposition of volatilized gases to soil and water. Emission factors of NH3 from chemical fertilizers, manure storage and spreading and animal housing and nitrate waterborne emissions were obtained from Cóndor et al. (2011). Phosphorus waterborne emissions were

estimated according to the budget farm-gate methodology re- ported in Dalgaard et al. (2008). Carbon sequestration was esti- mated according to Dollé et al. (2015). 20: The size and the main characteristics of dairy farms are shown in Table 2. The average occupied area of organic farms resulted significantly greater < 0.001) than that of conventional farms, but the variability in farm size was also greater, with farms varying from only a few dozens to more than three hundreds of hectares. Contributions of maize and grassland (namely alfalfa) to feed pro- duction were not different between the two samples of organic and conventional dairy farms; other crops, such as Italian ryegrass, wheat, barley, and in some cases sunflower or sugar beet were minor contributors in both systems. In both the organic and con- ventional farms, the contribution of grassland was extremely var- iable. No natural perennial meadows were found and animals did not have access to pasture in either in the conventional or organic farms; however, large exercise yards were available to heifers and lactating cows in the organic farms. Herd sizes did not differ be- tween conventional and organic farms, although the stocking rate was significantly lower (P <<0.01) in the conventional than in the organic farms. Yearly milk production per cow and per hectare was significantly higher in the conventional than in the organic farms (P < 0.05 and P 0.01, respectively); no differences were found in the number of culled cows and sold calves. The sample farms did not differ in their fossil fuel and energy consumption. Economic performances are reported in Table 3. Although milk production was lower in the organic farms, the incomes were not different between the two production systems, with a wide variability seen among the organic farms. The ratio between meat and milk reve- nues was significantly higher (P < 0.05) for the conventional farms

than the organic farms, where the milk price is much higher. However, there was no difference in meat price between the sample farm systems, because animals sold by organic dairy farms are not differentiated from those from conventional farms.

23: No differences were found in GWP, ACP, and EUP associated with 1 kg of FPCM produced in conventional or organic farms (Fig. 2), even when different allocation criteria were adopted. The only exception was ACP with physical allocation, which was higher for organic milk than for conventional milk. One result of interest was the finding that some organic farms exhibited GWP per 1 kg of FPCM that was lower than the mean of the conventional farms. These organic farms had a high rate of production per cow and their forage system was largely based on maize silage. Fig. 3 reports the results in relation to area occupied. Global warming potential, ACP, and EUP were significantly (P < 0.05 or less) higher in the con- ventional system than in the organic system, regardless the allo- cation criteria was considered.

The relationship between milk productivity per cow and the carbon footprint per unit of product and per hectare is widely recognized (Casey and Holden, 2005; Kristensen et al., 2011; O'Brien et al., 2014). In their previous work on dairy buffalos, Pirlo et al. (2014) demonstrated that milk productivity explains more than 72% of the variability in the carbon footprint of 1 kg of buffalo milk. Table 4 shows the regressions between environ- mental performances and milk production, expressed either as kg of FPCM per cow per year or as tons of FPCM per hectare. Emis- sions of GHG associated with 1 kg of FPCM are significantly (P < 0.05) reduced by the increase in average milk production per cow in conventional and organic system. Interestingly, the carbon footprint of 1 kg of FPCM responds to productivity in a similar way in conventional and organic system, suggesting that improvement of milk production per cow is an effective mitigation strategy in both systems. These observations are consistent with those made by Guerci et al. (2013a) and Kiefer et al. (2014), who reported that variation in milk productivity explains a large portion of GWP variability. Increasing milk production per cow also resulted in a significant (P < 0.05) increase of GHG gas emissions per ha in the conventional and organic system. However, there was no signifi- cant relationship between average milk production and ACP and EUP in regards to either 1 kg of FPCM or 1 ha in either production system. This is in contrast with the results of Guerci et al. (2013a) who reported some significant relationships between cows' pro- ductivity, ACP and EUP. Inputs per cow or inputs per kg of FPCM resulted in a significant correlation to GWP in both conventional and organic system (Table 5), a result that is consistent with O'Brien et al. (2014). However, we did not find any significant regression between ACP and EUP (Table Nevertheless, consumption of inputs per kilo- gram of FPCM explained a good share of the variability (R2 > 50%) in ACP and EUP in the conventional farms and EUP found in the in organic farms, indicating that consumption of inputs per cow or per kilogram of FPCM are not good indicators of ACP and EUP but are good drivers of environmental categories under some circum- stances. 29: In this study, average milk production per cow was lower in the organic farms than in conventional farms, which is consistent with the results of nearly all the studies reported in the literature (Table 1). The results concerning GWP, ACP, and EUP per unit of occupied land were also consistent with the literature: organic milk production is always associated with a reduction of GHG and pollutants per hectare. Nevertheless, the results are controversial in regards to the unit of product. Cederberg and Mattson (2000) and Guerci et al. (2013b) found lower GHG emissions per kilo- gram of milk when the organic system was characterized by a reduced use of fossil fuels for crop growth. However, Liang (2017) showed that shifting from an intensive to an extensive system could result in an increase of the GHG per kilogram of product. Organic and conventional farming are two very large categories that are performed in a variety of ways in different regions, and production systems and environmental performances conse- quently can vary.

When considering carbon sequestration, Hörtenhuber et al. (2010) calculated that conversion to an organic system led to an appreciable increase of soil carbon stock and a consequent reduc- tion of the carbon footprint. However, these results were obtained in an environment where the proportion of permanent grassland was larger than that where the present study was conducted.

Contribution of different farm activities to GWP did not differ between the two systems (Table with the exclusion of inputs for cropping (encompassing CO2 emissions for producing synthetic fertilizer, seeds, pesticides, and plastic) which were significantly higher (P < 0.001) in the conventional farms than in the organic farms. In accordance with Guerci et al. (2013b) and Pirlo et al. (2014), the main contributor to GWP was CH4 from enteric fermentation, followed by purchased feeds and direct or indirect N2O emitted from manure applied to the soil or following N rede- position. The soil carbon budget result was negative and did not differ between organic and conventional system. While wide variability among farms in terms of the soil carbon budget was observed, its contribution was, on average, very small in the two samples, because the forage systems were similar and were both based on maize and temporary grasslands in both cases. The contribution of carbon emissions or sequestration largely depends on the forage system. Annual crops characterize intensive systems, which are the only forages; therefore, carbon sequestration is limited. Knapp et al. (2014) summarized several strategies for reducing enteric CH4 emissions. Some of them are still at the experimental stage or cannot be adopted under the organic system, but most of them can also be pursued in the organic system, such as improvements in forage quality and feed processing. Selection for productive and efficient animals is also a strategy used to reduce CH4 emission intensity (de Haas et al., 2017); the organic farms included in the present study that had the lowest milk carbon footprints also exhibited the highest productivity and maize silage was a key ingredient in the diets of their animals.

Greenhouse gas emissions can also be reduced by improving manure management and its application. An efficient use of manure is a very effective strategy in conventional system for reducing direct emissions of N20 and indirect emissions of CO2 because it allows for the reduction of chemical fertilizers. However, there are several strategies that can also be applied in the organic system. The main strategy is to reduce the dietary protein content, but only to an extent such that animal health and milk production are not damaged (Hristov al., 2013). It is easier to reduce dietary protein content without reducing milk production in conventional system, because solvent-extracted soybean meal and protected amino acids can be utilized; in the organic system, on the contrary, the use of solvent-extracted meal and protected amino acids is not permitted and this represents a factor that limits the productivity of cows under this system. Contribution of different farm activities to GWP did not differ between the two systems (Table with the exclusion of inputs for cropping (encompassing CO2 emissions for producing synthetic fertilizer, seeds, pesticides, and plastic) which were significantly higher (P < 0.001) in the conventional farms than in the organic farms. In accordance with Guerci et al. (2013b) and Pirlo et al. (2014), the main contributor to GWP was CH4 from enteric fermentation, followed by purchased feeds and direct or indirect N2O emitted from manure applied to the soil or following N rede- position. The soil carbon budget result was negative and did not differ between organic and conventional system. While wide variability among farms in terms of the soil carbon budget was observed, its contribution was, on average, very small in the two samples, because the forage systems were similar and were both based on maize and temporary grasslands in both cases. The contribution of carbon emissions or sequestration largely depends on the forage system. Annual crops characterize intensive systems, which are the only forages; therefore, carbon sequestration is limited. Knapp et al. (2014) summarized several strategies for reducing enteric CH4 emissions. Some of them are still at the experimental stage or cannot be adopted under the organic system, but most of them can also be pursued in the organic system, such as improvements in forage quality and feed processing. Selection for productive and efficient animals is also a strategy used to reduce CH4 emission intensity (de Haas et al., 2017); the organic farms included in the present study that had the lowest milk carbon footprints also exhibited the highest productivity and maize silage was a key ingredient in the diets of their animals.

Greenhouse gas emissions can also be reduced by improving manure management and its application. An efficient use of manure is a very effective strategy in conventional system for reducing direct emissions of N20 and indirect emissions of CO2 because it allows for the reduction of chemical fertilizers. However, there are several strategies that can also be applied in the organic system. The main strategy is to reduce the dietary protein content, but only to an extent such that animal health and milk production are not damaged (Hristov al., 2013). It is easier to reduce dietary protein content without reducing milk production in conventional system, because solvent-extracted soybean meal and protected amino acids can be utilized; in the organic system, on the contrary, the use of solvent-extracted meal and protected amino acids is not permitted and this represents a factor that limits the productivity of cows under this system. Strategies to reduce ACP are very similar to those adopted for N20. Two factors regulate NH3 emissions from manure and soil: the amount of N excreted and the management and utilization of manure. However, it is difficult to improve the dietary protein ef- ficiency in organic systems because of the constrains in utilization of soybean and feed additives. Consequently, the most suitable strategy should rely on techniques that reduce NH3 losses and improve the amount of N that can be utilized by crops. For example, manure can be buried immediately after its distribution in the field or precision-farming techniques can be adopted to apply it at the most appropriate time and rate.

There are two main activities that share their effects on EUP from milk production (Table 8): manure management and appli- cation and purchased feeds. As in the case of ACP, the organic system's manure management and application contributed signif- icantly (P < 0.001) more to EUP than did the conventional system,

because chemical fertilizers were not the sources of N input. Excluding the utilization of some dietary protein sources, such as soybean meal and protected essential amino acids that permit reduction in dietary protein without reducing milk production, strategies for reducing EUP are similar in organic and conventional systems. They mainly rely on timing manure or fertilizers appli- cation with vegetable growth and on improvement of the efficiency in the use of feeds produced on farms.
3.5. How and how much can organic milk production reduce environmental impact?

The results of this and other studies have shown that the GWP, ACP, and EUP of one unit of milk produced by organic systems is not different from that produced by conventional systems; but the environmental burden associated with one unit of occupied land is undoubtedly lower. To understand if organic farming can improve the sustainability, some general considerations are required. Global warming is an impact category with a global relevance, because its environmental effect is unaffected by local conditions and is not limited to the geographical area from which GHG are emitted. In contrast, there are some other environmental categories, such as acidification and eutrophication, for which the sources of emissions are more relevant.

Strategies to reduce ACP are very similar to those adopted for N20. Two factors regulate NH3 emissions from manure and soil: the amount of N excreted and the management and utilization of manure. However, it is difficult to improve the dietary protein ef- ficiency in organic systems because of the constrains in utilization of soybean and feed additives. Consequently, the most suitable strategy should rely on techniques that reduce NH3 losses and improve the amount of N that can be utilized by crops. For example, manure can be buried immediately after its distribution in the field or precision-farming techniques can be adopted to apply it at the most appropriate time and rate.

There are two main activities that share their effects on EUP from milk production (Table 8): manure management and appli- cation and purchased feeds. As in the case of ACP, the organic system's manure management and application contributed signif- icantly (P < 0.001) more to EUP than did the conventional system,

because chemical fertilizers were not the sources of N input. Excluding the utilization of some dietary protein sources, such as soybean meal and protected essential amino acids that permit reduction in dietary protein without reducing milk production, strategies for reducing EUP are similar in organic and conventional systems. They mainly rely on timing manure or fertilizers appli- cation with vegetable growth and on improvement of the efficiency in the use of feeds produced on farms.
3.5. How and how much can organic milk production reduce environmental impact?

The results of this and other studies have shown that the GWP, ACP, and EUP of one unit of milk produced by organic systems is not different from that produced by conventional systems; but the environmental burden associated with one unit of occupied land is undoubtedly lower. To understand if organic farming can improve the sustainability, some general considerations are required. Global warming is an impact category with a global relevance, because its environmental effect is unaffected by local conditions and is not limited to the geographical area from which GHG are emitted. In contrast, there are some other environmental categories, such as acidification and eutrophication, for which the sources of emissions are more relevant.

47: This study has confirmed that the environmental impact of organic milk production is more favourable than that of con- ventional systems in regard to unit of land occupied. It has also shown that there is no difference between the organic and conventional systems when the environmental impact refers to 1 kg of FPCM. Most of the farms, in both the organic and con- ventional samples, relied on maize and temporal grasslands and the average milk production per cows was higher in the con- ventional than in the organic farms. Therefore, the advantages due to a reduced amount of inputs, such as purchased feeds and chemical fertilizers, are negatively compensated by lower milk productivity.

An analysis of the relationships between environmental performances and some main farm characteristics highlighted the strong effect of cow productivity on GWP per kilogram of FPCM. The models reported in this study estimated the effect of milk production improvement in reducing GWP and showed that it is similar in both systems. The study also suggests that there were some organic farms producing as much milk as the average production level seen in conventional farms and that they had similar environmental performances on an FPCM basis. Regression analysis did not give the same results for ACP and EUP.

Although organic dairy production systems were shown to have a lower environmental impact on occupied area basis, this study does not suggest organic farming would be an effective strategy to reduce some environmental categories with relevant local interest, such as acidification and eutrophication. A consequential LCA analysis should be made to determine whether a shift from con- ventional to an organic dairy production system has beneficial ef- fects on air and water quality in the northern regions of Italy.

Further studies are also needed to examine different environ- mental characteristics relevant to organic systems, which could have an important impact on consumers' choices and political de- cisions, such as human- and eco-toxicity and biodiversity.

It remains to be seen if the adoption of some practices, including grazing and a larger contribution of permanent meadows, could improve the environmental categories considered in this study. It is noteworthy that the farms characterized by a larger proportion of grasslands and a smaller proportion of maize also had a lower milk production per cow. Most likely, this association does not have a physiological basis, but, rather, is due to strategic made by farmers, who consider not only incomes from milk but also ecological ser- vices (Pirlo et al., 2017). The presence of organic dairy farms with cows producing as much milk as those of conventional farms means that it is also possible to reduce the environmental impact associated with one unit of product.
